title: Limp binding with vellum wrappers
subtitle: A historically-rooted binding in which dampened vellum wraps and firms the text block
excerpt: Limp vellum was the dominant binding of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. There are no boards: the vellum wrapper itself supports the book, hardening as it dries around the text block and settling to a characteristic warped cover. Making one teaches the material logic of the book before the age of pasteboard.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on early bookbinding structures and limp bindings. Cyril Davenport, The Book: Its History and Development (1907). Both public domain; Internet Archive scans available. The historical production method for limp vellum is well-documented in both sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding without rigid boards in which the cover material itself provides the structural support by wrapping tightly around the text block.
Prepared animal skin, traditionally calfskin, stretched and scraped thin. When dampened and allowed to dry under tension it firms and holds its shape.
A knotted thread or leather tie passed through the cover and the sewing structure to hold the limp wrapper in place.

content.text: A
limp binding
dispenses with boards altogether. The text block is sewn in the usual way, but instead of being cased into stiff boards the sewing is attached directly to a wrapper of dampened
vellum
, which is then laced through with
tackets
and left to dry around the book. The vellum firms as it dries, producing a robust but flexible cover that wears and warps with use.
Vellum is stiff when dry. Dampen it only as needed and keep the workshop temperature stable while drying. Avoid sharp folds in dry vellum as it will crack rather than crease.
Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Keep the non-cutting hand behind the blade path when trimming vellum.
What you need
Text block: 80 gsm, grain running parallel to the spine, sewn on tapes or cords. Twelve to twenty signatures of four to six leaves each is a comfortable size.

Vellum: a single piece of modern paper vellum or prepared calfskin vellum, cut to twice the height of the text block plus 30 mm and three times the board width. Real skin vellum is authentic; paper vellum is more accessible for a first attempt.
Thread: waxed linen thread for the tackets. Pass thread through beeswax twice before using.
Preparing and dampening the vellum

Cut the vellum to size. For real skin, dampen the flesh side lightly with a sponge and allow the moisture to penetrate for 5 minutes before working. Vellum in a limp binding does not need to be soaking wet; it needs enough moisture to fold cleanly at the spine.
Wrapping and tacket-lacing
Place the damp vellum on the bench. Centre the text block spine-down on the vellum's mid-line.
Bring the front wrapper up and over the text block, then the back wrapper, folding them neatly at head and tail.
Pierce through the cover and the spine sewing at three or five evenly spaced points using the bookbinding awl. The piercing points should align with the tapes or cords of the sewing structure.
Thread the linen thread through each pair of holes, knotting on the inside to form a tacket at each station. Pull the knots snug but not so tight the vellum puckers.
Place the book spine-down on a flat surface and allow the vellum to dry for at least 4 hours at room temperature. Do not force-dry with heat. The vellum will firm around the text block as it dries.
Finishing

Once fully dry, trim the fore-edge flap to a consistent width and fold it over neatly. Tie the traditional vellum closure: a length of cord or vellum thong threaded through slits in the fore-edge flap and wound around the book.
Care

Store away from direct heat and sunlight, which cause vellum to cockle and shrink. Do not store a limp vellum book in a closed box without air circulation. If the cover warps with seasonal humidity changes, this is normal behaviour: vellum is a hygroscopic material and the warping is not a repair issue.
